title: Tech firm FiEE says it will spend $3m to expand into AI music - Music Ally
text:
The debate about whether AI-generated music is unfairly sluicing royalties away from human artists and songwriters is in full swing. But where there’s money, there’ll be more companies looking to take a share of it.
FiEE is the latest,
announcing plans to invest around $3m to get into the AI-music game
. $51k of that is being spent to buy a 51% stake in a company called YinLian Culture, with the rest being made available as a loan to help it develop its ‘Maltose Culture’ AI-music platform.
Who are these companies, though? The announcement describes Maltose Culture as “a leader in China’s vibrant music landscape” with “partnerships with China’s leading digital platforms” around music and karaoke.
As for FiEE, it’s a Hong Kong-based “technology company integrating IoT, connectivity, and AI to redefine brand management solutions in the digital era” which has now targeted expansion into the music business.
This isn’t Suno-level financial muscle.
FiEE generated $6.2m of revenue in the whole of 2025
, most of which came from its move into ‘MCN digital services’.
Still, focusing on China is logical, with AI-generated music growing rapidly there.
Indeed, last week China Daily
reported
on some of the money being made, leading with a programmer who’d just “sold the rights to an artificial intelligence-generated song he had created in just about two hours for 50,000 yuan ($7,238) to a company”.
Want a bleak contextualisation of that figure in terms of creative effort? You’d need
more than 1.5m streams of a song on Spotify in the US
to generate the same amount in royalties.
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
